title: 1.e4 — The King's Pawn
summary: An overview of every major 1.e4 reply

steps.title: The Romantic Opening
steps.text:
**{{e4}}** is the most popular first move in the history of chess. Bobby Fischer called it *best by test*, and he had a point — the move does five things at once.

## What 1.e4 accomplishes

- **Claims a center square** — e4 is one of the four key squares.
- **Frees the queen** along the d1-h5 diagonal.
- **Frees the f1 bishop** along the a6-f1 diagonal.
- **Threatens nothing yet** — but creates instant possibilities.
- **Sets the tone** — open, tactical games are far more common after 1.e4 than 1.d4.

## What White generally wants

- Get pieces out fast (knights to f3 and c3).
- Pressure key squares like **f7** and **e5**.
- Castle short and use the e-file for rook activity.

## Why beginners should try it

Games after 1.e4 tend to be open, with clearer tactics and faster decisions. Practising 1.e4 sharpens your tactical eye in a way that slow d4 systems often don't.

> 1.e4 is the bright morning sun of chess — open lines, lively pieces, real fights.

steps.title: Black's Main Replies
steps.text:
Black has a rich menu of replies to 1.e4 — each defining a different chess universe.

## The big six

- **1...e5** — *Open Games*. Mirroring White's claim. Leads to the Italian, Ruy Lopez, Scotch, King's Gambit, Petroff, and more.
- **1...c5** — *Sicilian Defense*. The sharpest, most popular fighter's choice. Asymmetric, dynamic, full of theory.
- **1...e6** — *French Defense*. Solid and strategic. Black builds a pawn chain after **...d5**.
- **1...c6** — *Caro-Kann Defense*. Quiet and bulletproof. Prepares ...d5 with c-pawn support.
- **1...d5** — *Scandinavian Defense*. Direct: trade pawns immediately. Easy to learn.
- **1...Nf6** — *Alekhine's Defense*. Hypermodern provocation. Tempts White's pawns forward to attack them later.

## Less common but legitimate

- **1...d6** — *Pirc / Modern* — letting White build, then striking back.
- **1...g6** — *Modern Defense* — fianchetto first, decide later.
- **1...Nc6** — *Nimzowitsch Defense* — quirky but playable.

## How to choose what to play

- *Tactical, want to attack?* — Open Game with 1...e5 or Sicilian Najdorf.
- *Hate memorizing?* — Caro-Kann or Pirc.
- *Love positional play?* — French or Caro-Kann.

> Pick one defense and learn it deeply. Five strong systems beats fifty shallow ones.

steps.prompt: Meet 1.e4 with the classical reply — play **{{e5}}** and stake your own claim in the center.
steps.hint: 1...e5 is the time-honored answer. It mirrors White and opens the same diagonals for your bishop and queen.
